comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting toward midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me and the teachers who influenced them in turn. Three weeks ago I started on the OBOD end of my lineage with past Chosen Chief Philip Carr-Gomm. Last week we got to Vera Chapman, and that brings us this week to John Hargrave of the Kindred of the Kibbo Kift. White Fox, to give him his Kin name, was a student of Ernest Thompson Seton, the Canadian artist and naturalist who founded the Woodcraft movement in 1902. The Kibbo Kift -- the name's a bit of Kentish dialect meaning "proof of great strength" -- was founded by Hargrave and a circle of Boy Scout leaders with Woodcraft connections who were sickened by the intensely militaristic flavor of Scouting at that time. The Kindred attracted a great many young men and women in the postwar years and sketched out many of the themes that became central to the appropriate tech and natural-living movements in later decades. Hargrave also wrote a series of novels and nonfiction works. People who knew him -- including Vera Chapman -- noted that he had a considerable background in occultism. The Depression and the Second World War put paid to the Kindred, but there has been a considerable revival of interest in the Kibbo Kift in recent years; it (and Seton's Woodcraft movement generally) are among the early 20th century movements worth revisiting. That's a group of the Kinfolk on parade below on the right.

[IMAGE:1]Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

comments.content:
I saw a couple posts around the web recently about people who are sexually attracted to certain religious depictions of the virgin Mary. It made me wonder, how is sexual attraction to gods and other holy beings handled in the various religions, assuming they speak of it at all? I doubt Christianity or the other Abrahamic faiths would approve of it given their usual attitude towards sex, but I think I remember you saying that arousal which springs from worship is spoken of euphemistically in some texts on Christian mysticism. I'm guessing other faiths would be more open to at least discussing it based on how often the gods take on human lovers in mythology.

Also, what advice if any would you (or other posters) give to someone in this situation? I assume praying to the deity in question for guidance would be the main thing, yes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It really depends on the religion. Even within polytheist faiths, there's a lot of variation. One common thread, however, is that getting all hot and bothered about virgin goddesses is a very, very bad idea; that was how Actaeon ended up being torn to pieces by his own dogs, for example.

Yes, you should pray to the deity in question. You might also find some more appropriate focus for your desires.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Sexually attracted" is ambiguous, but in case this helps, I have read one account by someone who unexpectedly drained the insistent force out of a cluster of paraphilias using the identity-memory reconsolidation technique described in the book "Transforming Your Self: Becoming who you want to be", written by an advanced practitioner from the neuro-linguistic programming tradition:

https://kajsotala.fi/2017/07/how-i-found-fixed-the-root-problem-behind-my-depression-and-anxiety-after-20-years/ (the title of the post is a historical artifact that oversells what happened, but he properly walks it back in the postscripts)

I'm not clear on how that's supposed to work, but reading that and some related accounts, my guess is that there's some part of people that can choose to create certain internal artificial cravings or psychological privations of "okayness", to power things like paraphilias and shame triggers, to try to manipulate the person into doing particular things. If someone fixes whatever problem it is that this part of themselves is trying to solve, sometimes it decides to stop powering the other downstream psychological problems. In this case, the problem had something to do with un-integrated identity-memories that led to an expectation of ultimately not being accepted.

Steve Andreas, the author of the book, suggests on his web page for the book that people also read someone else's book called "Core Transformation: Unlocking the Wellspring Within", which is about a related (and probably hazardous, like a power tool) ritual-like introspection technique that supposedly gets that part of oneself to revisit its reasoning: instead of wanting yourself to do a thing and therefore using some kind of internal privation of "okayness" to manipulate yourself into doing it, how might it work to just have the okayness anyway, and separately do the thing because you want yourself to do it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Tibetan Buddhism provides a nice example - some Tantric goddesses indicate their non-celibate state from the beginning, but Tara in her typical form is celibate; if you visualize yourself in sexual union, you would be forcing her to break her vows! She's very much the 'virgin goddess' there. But if you take her Tantric initiation, you *must* visualize her in sexual union, and can also practice by visualizing either yourself as her, or your partner as her, while in physical sexual union. Part of this has to do with symbolic meanings that must be unpacked by analytical meditation, part of it with the experiences or glimpses of nonduality during initiation; another part has to do with transformation and deification of one's sexual chakras; another part has to do with the physical and etheric embodiments of bliss, easily accessible to us through the sexual chakras, which can be then used to build or reshape the etheric body through Tantric yoga practices.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Becoming sexually enamored of a divinity is not a great idea. They are unlikely to be any more flattered by our attentions than we would be by the lustings of caterpillars or sea slugs. We're really not of their caliber in any way. Most divinities do appreciate our admiration, awe, wonder, worship, and love but not so much our carnal obsession. Of course, there are exceptions, such as gods of lust and cravings, but many of those it would not be at all wise to let have that kind of influence over you.

Gods are also perfectly capable of protecting themselves from our unwanted yearnings, so beware should any sexual fantasies with a divinity appear to come to fruition. Your unappreciated cravings could easily have been redirected towards a demon or some other non-divine being that you would not ever want to get intimate with, given that it can take well more than a lifetime to clear the damage they can inflict once invited in.

On the other hand, should some divinity unexpectedly take a hankering after you, there's not much you can do to stop them from having their way. The exchange of vital energy that divine coitus reflects gets perceived by us in our human bodies as a sexual act. Who knows how the gods themselves perceive that kind of sharing of potency. For humans to feel aroused in the presence of divinity is quite natural, as they are incredibly powerful; however, lusting after or craving their immense power is a recipe for disaster.

If some divinity does decide to share his or her potency with you, they probably have very good reasons for making that choice. Be sure to thank them for their gift and ask them what they would like you to do with the potency they have chosen to incarnate through you. And, of course, pray that that won't in any way end in crucifixion — hey, it's been known to happen before! Divine nookie isn't always what it's cracked up to be.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Spengler's "Soul Image" and Question on Turning Fire's Passion into Will
replies.content:
This doesn't take the form of a practical guide, but a reasonable hypothesis about the intrinsic root mechanism of how there could be a "willpower" that were not just an aggregate of passions, and about of the relationship of such a willpower to the passions, is sketched in George Ainslie's book "Breakdown of Will", which he condenses here:

https://www.picoeconomics.org/HTarticles/Bkdn_Precis/Precis2.html

Ainslie's hypothesis is complex and hard to summarize, since it depends on a game-theoretic strategic calculation going on between the passions of multiple stages of the self at different points in time. It also depends on certain only-half-stated premises about how brains make decisions about how to bundle different possibilities together (including decisions and bundlings) when self-predicting. It's easier to follow if you're familiar with some ideas from reinforcement learning and Hayekian ways of thinking about systems. The parts about bundling are easier to follow if you're familiar with some ideas from machine learning, even though the book is from 2001.

Some notable quotes in the the full book (Ainslie presents the quotes from other authors as being correct, as far as they go, but not painting a complete enough picture):

(from David Hume) "Nothing can oppose or retard the impulse of passion but a contrary impulse."

"One significant advance was Francis Bacon's realization that reason didn't have its own force, but had to get its way by playing one passion against another: It had to 'set affection against affection and to master one by another: even as we use to hunt beast with beast. . . . For as in the government of states it is sometimes necessary to bridle one faction with another, so it is in the government within.'"

"The ability to take a drink at New Years or go off the diet on Thanksgiving provides flexibility to a potentially rigid commitment to a concrete rule; but the same principle that keeps Thanksgiving from setting a precedent might also work for my birthday and, with decreasing credibility, for the Fourth of July, St. Patrick's Day, Labor Day, Arbor Day, St. Swithin's Day, and Just This Once. That kind of logic can degrade a personal rule without my ever breaking it."

(Notably, that kind of slippery-slope scenario is part of the way "decisions about whether to break a rule" end up bleeding together with "decisions about how to bundle different possibilities together". Because of this, rule-breaking and rule-gerrymandering both end up being intuitively bundled as the same kind of possibility, even though they're options in different decisions: rule-breaking is an option about what to externally do, while rule-gerrymandering is "only" an option about what internal bundling of decisions to use. Going further, you can include socially defined bundlings to shore up self-defined bundlings, thereby getting a kind of social willpower, that also bundles "authority-undermining" and "norm-subversion" together with "rule-gerrymandering" and "rule-breaking".

Importantly, this further bundling is an absolutely critical core mechanism of pathological fundamentalisms and Puritanisms and authoritarianisms. If you ever find yourself having uncharitable feelings towards critical theorists, consider thinking of the semi-true idea that the founders of the field of critical studies were motivated by seeing that big non-obvious thing, and trying to figure out how to indicate it, subvert the power bases it had infected, and one day forever defeat it, supposing the future freedom or enslavement of all humanity to have been at stake, since this was during the frightening 20th century rise of totalitarianism. There just happened to be some unforeseen enantiodromia afterwards.)

In a discussion of the introspective experience of free will: "Some cognitivists acknowledge that cognitions must act by brokering emotions, as the philosopher d'Holbach first described it two hundred years ago: 'Reason . . . is nothing but the act of choosing those passions which we must follow for the sake of our happiness.' But this still requires that part of the psyche that is the broker to move passions but be unmoved by them. This ego is like a sailor or canoeist, who navigates by balancing forces stronger than her own, but still does so with some force not derived from the wind or current.

If I'm shooting rapids in a canoe, the force of the water is much stronger than my arms. I can choose only which current will bear me along. But the strength I steer with is still my own and is unrelated to the force of the water. To model strict utility theory, a canoeist would also have to steer by the force of the rushing water, which would mean that the rationale of the steering choices would somehow have to use the logic of rushing water, not the extraneous choices of a person floating on it. [...] Such a description has, in effect, to evoke the feeling of being a canoeist from the logic of the water -- a challenge perhaps as great as proposing how life itself arose from inorganic matter."

(He goes on to suggest how something like this is nonetheless plausible, though you have to go outside the realm of utility theory. That last line suggests an alchemical meditation theme about willpower vs. passions vs. life vs. matter.)

Ainslie's hypothesis isn't just the obvious one about how people's passions care less about faraway pleasures and pains than impending ones. The simplest way for that to work wouldn't leave any room for conflict between one's current passions and one's future passions about tradeoffs involving further-future pleasures and pains. Without that conflict, there wouldn't be any angle of incentive for your current passions to willingly let themselves get harvested, into powering an independent center of willpower to constrain your nearer-future self's passions. And your nearer-future self's passions would have no reason of their own to go along with that scheme and perpetuate it. The game-theoretic strategic calculations have to be twistier than that.

Ainslie's explanations about pathologies of the will are particularly noteworthy; they're sort of the answer to the "Chesterton's fence" question that has been posed by nature, in the fact that we can't just have our willpower always overrule all of our passions right from the beginning.

If the principle described by the book is as fundamental as it sounds, reading the whole book and taking it as a source of meditation themes might contribute to a certain kind of inner power. Although, even if that was true, it would presumably only work if there were also some additional balancing and complementary influences, and I wouldn't know where to look for those. (Well. I mean of course you'd ideally also bring to the table a thorough familiarity with the way decision theory hypothetically works for the utility-maximizing Homo economicus that humans aren't, and with mechanism design, institutional economics, and public choice theory. But those feel obvious and like they're almost just a redundant suggestion with meditating on the book.)

My own current takeaway from Ainslie's argument is not "willpower is awesome, I want to have all of it". Rather, it is, "it looks like there's an intrinsic limit on what I can achieve while trapped within the paradigm of 'willpower' vs. 'impulsiveness'. I want to become something that can reflect on the different forces whose negotiations use my psyche and consciousness as one of their mediums, and... "... and... And I don't have words for what I want to be able to do from that position, since it doesn't involve a straightforward kind of selfhood. But it has something to do with coherent principles, fairness, proportionate concessions, eventual proportionate safety from both the dangers that bright-line boundaries protect against and the dramas and entanglements that bright-line boundaries bring with them, gains from trade, reality-groundedness, subsidiarity (in something like a Catholic or Hayekian sense), and sustainability.

I don't know. Maybe the above paragraph's posited "post-willpower" sort of state would be unattainable to normal humans, or not worth the cost, or could generally only safely be reached by passing through a stage of having an actual well-cultivated regular willpower first. Maybe I haven't understood what "willpower" was always supposed to have truly meant from the beginning.

I haven't given much thought to how this perspective on willpower relates to the soul, except that I imagine that whatever's arranging for the soul to be a thing probably wants the consequences of operating one's material thoughts in particular ways to be good teaching proxies for the consequences of operating one's non-material thoughts in particular ways.

Thank you for sharing this, I'm interested to read at least the precis, and perhaps the whole book. It also strikes me as somewhat amusing that the bleeding edge of economic decision theory seems to be arriving at similar conclusions from the various occult traditions I'm learning about (the different parts of the Soul posited in some Heathen works, the different influences of the planets and signs in astrology, the different elements/humors as drivers within the personality, and so forth).

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Yesterday I wandered around a forest alone for an hour. I tried to clear my mind, and to go through the forest meditatively. I kept feeling a shift between getting swept up in my thoughts, which really felt like I was going somewhere else, and catching this, and ending up almost completely 'there' in the forest. I noticed that voice of inner thought still quietly trying to come in, and I kept pushing it away, and eventually I got the hang of it, and tried to really look at everything around me. All felt totally quiet, and I felt like I was moving through the forest almost soundlessly, or at least that I could choose to be soundless or not.

There was a strange distance between me and the forest I was looking at, almost like my plane of vision was a sheet of some unknown material, and my arm looked slightly unfamiliar as it stretched into that plane of vision.

I remember sometimes being pulled into thought, literally being lost in thought, that my consciousness would whoosh off somewhere else and I'd be caught completely in it. I eventually toward the end of my walk got tired, and couldn't really hold the attentiveness anymore.

What was happening there? Was this mindfulness meditation, or just a forcing of myself to sense more rather than think? I do discursive meditation daily, but this felt different.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There are many different states of consciousness. The one you experienced is one of them, and yes, it's different from discursive meditation! That state of attentive, silent perception is called "single awareness" in some Western occult traditions, and it can be useful to cultivate.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks! Not that this state of consciousness was anything close, but your reply made me wonder if there are different types of enlightenment, enlightenments in the plural, rather than a single state of consciousness?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Exactly. The further you go along the spiritual path, the more irreducibly individual the path becomes. The point of spiritual development is not to dissolve our unique individuality into a vast pot of divine goo; it's to unfold our individual potential, stage by stage by stage, until we become what the Divine meant us to be all along -- and that's unique to each soul.

I've been waiting for this one for a long time, and I'm surely going to order a copy on Amazon one of these days. I'm from a Smarta Hindu family, and the theme of this book resonates with me very well, because Smarta Hinduism is the only one of the four major denominations among the Hindu family that holds the view that there are multiple equal supreme deities - if I'm not mistaken, this was the worldview of the ancient Greeks, too. I have nothing against the other three denominations (Shaiva, Shakta and Vaishnava); but there is indeed a qualitative difference between the Smarta religious worldview on the one hand, and Shaiva on the other, which considers Shiva as the only supreme deity, and that all other deities are created by Shiva (similarly, you have Vishnu for the Vaishnavas and Devi for the Shaktas) - the Smarta view, however, is that all the six supreme deities (Surya, Shiva, Vishnu, Ganesha, Skanda and Devi) originate in a higher reality, but at a functional level are equivalent (not equal, though) and independent living beings worthy of worship.

Much thanks again, and best wishes!

comments.title: Whole sign or Placidus
comments.content:
I know natal astrology isn't your focus but I was wondering which system you find more accurate/useful when interpreting your own chart. I've read a few astrologers saying that wholesign was the traditional method for reading natal charts yet placings is the default on most astrology calculators.

In my own case, wholesign puts my sun in the fourth house rather than the third, venus in the fifth rather than the fourth, and part of fortune in the tenth rather than the ninth. So some pretty decent differences between the two if we're going to use one or the other.

So which house system have you found to work better, or is it more of an 'overlap' thing?

replies.title: Re: Whole sign or Placidus
replies.content:
(Op)

Some typos in there. 'Placidus' not 'placings', and '...pretty decent differences between the two if I WERE going to use one or the other.'

replies.title: Re: Whole sign or Placidus
replies.content: To my mind, whole sign houses are a blunt instrument that deprives an astrological chart of much of its meaning. Intercepted signs and signs that cover more than one house -- impossible in a whole sign system -- are important, and have a lot to teach about personality and destiny! I use Placidus houses in all my astrological work, though I've also experimented with Regiomontanus and Koch houses as well as whole sign. I find that Placidus gets me more accurate results.

replies.title: Re: Whole sign or Placidus
replies.content:
I've gotten very, very good results out of Hellenistic astrology, which uses whole signs, and decent ones with modern astrology, where I typically used Placidus, as well as Renaissance astrology, where my default was Regiomontanus; but the meanings of the houses are different in each school of astrology, in some cases substantially so. Each one also factor in certain subtle things which the others ignore or do not allow; such as varying midheaven houses in Hellenistic Astrology holding a great deal of meaning.

Any time I tried to use a different house system than the one the school used though, the results have consistently been somewhere between less than ideal and terrible. So my advice is to use whichever house system the school of astrology you are looking at uses.

replies.title: Re: Whole sign or Placidus
replies.content: That strikes me as very good advice.

Hi, JMG—

Thank you for offering to answer all the various and sundry questions!

I am currently trying to decide between starting with Learning Ritual Magic or Gareth Knight’s Experience of the Inner Worlds. First, from my cursory read-through of both, it seems like EIW only touches on some ideas (i.e, Pathways, Tarot trumps)—and where it does mention them, it’s as if there’s an expectation that the reader already has a level of experience or familiarity with them. Whereas in LRM, the knowledge/practice is given with the assumption that the student is starting from square one and the ideas are built out as you progress. Does EIW need Knight’s other books as companions to round out the lessons?

Second, the descriptions of the exercises in EIW are very general when compared to LRM or CGD—in fact, the lack of specificity as compared to LRM and CGD is marked. Is that a concern? Is there an assumption that the student already has familiarity/practice with this kind of work?

Last, it seems like the single goal of EIW is the scrying. It also seems like there is the expectation that the practitioner will be working with a group. Is this accurate? After EIW, what would a practitioner move on to, if not group work (which seems to be only available to UK residents or those who can fly there regularly)?

In short, I feel more drawn to LRM because I really like how systematic and fully fleshed-out as a system it feels—but I am drawn to EIW for its Christian emphasis.

Any help you can offer would be very appreciated!
-J

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
EIW was originally issued as the entrance to a correspondence course Knight offered. I have no idea if the course is still being offered now that Knight has passed on. It's intended for complete beginners, but you're right that the exercises are not as detailed as they might be. It does have the expectation that you live in Britain and will have no trouble getting involved in the author's occult group, but that's very common in British occult books. Britain's a very small country!

LRM was originally a correspondence course itself, and my co-authors and I aimed it at total beginners. We had a fair amount of experience in teaching beginners by then, so we made the practical instructions as detailed as they were because we knew how many people needed that. Later, when it was converted into a book, we revised it to fix problems our students had encountered.

As for what to do, that really does have to be your decision! Remember that you can do one and then the other!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In The Refiner's Fire, there was some discussion of the search for the "pure" Adamic language and its ties to the mythology of Enoch built up around Royal Arch Freemasonry. Understanding that many of these myths and stories carry symbolic rather than literal truths, what is your understanding of the symbolism of the Adamic language and the Enochian stories of Freemasonry?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I see the Adamic language as a myth -- to quote Sallustius, one of those stories that never happened but always are. I don't think there ever was a true original language, but it makes a good mythic representation of the archetype of language itself.

As for the Enochian legends, they're an example of that curious habit of esoteric traditions, the backdating of a historical event. There's tolerably good evidence that the Knights Templar explored the tunnels and chambers inside the Temple Mount, and they seem to have found things there -- though it's anyone's guess what. (I've speculated on what it might have been in my book The Secret of the Temple, but I don't have proof of my guess.) Since there does seem to have been some knowledge passed on from the Templars to Scottish Freemasonry, I think a good case can be made that the Templar discoveries are what's behind the Enochian legend, but here again we're not in Adamic territory; it's more likely that what was hidden dated from the fall of the Second Temple, not from any more antediluvian period.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What is the purpose of such myths?  I’m trying to understand what useful information they could convey.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's central to the nature of myths that what they communicate cannot be expressed in any more straightforward way. That's why the ancients put these things in the form of myths. Don't worry about it; simply allow the myths room in your thoughts, and see where they lead you.

I am familiar with the Old Hag experience (as described in your book Monsters).

A couple of nights ago, I woke up in the middle of the night and saw my wife was awake. She said she'd been woken up by what sounded like a typical hagging attack. comments.title: Awareness and Grief
comments.content:
First, to anyone getting started in their practice (whatever that may be,) I've learned to treasure the baby steps, memorization, and the complexities and difficulties faced when beginning. After some time with the SoP and meditation (among other practices,) I've noticed that having everything memorized and flowing requires even more focus and commitment than what was needed to get started. I look back at the first weeks and can see just how much change occurred within me and around me in those moments as I was benefited both by the novelty of doing something new, and the focus required to memorize and perform something unfamiliar.

Undertaking these practices, I've noticed that I have become more sensitive and aware on multiple levels. In training my mind, I now see how much I allowed distraction and numbing techniques to assuage the grief of looking at humans and other beings in various states of severe imbalance and suffering, and a natural world being vigorously consumed and controlled. Now, I find that I often feel grief rather than comfort from the distractions and numbing practices themselves, as they increasingly appear as the thin veil they always were. Also, my practices have brought me into closer relationship with the seen and unseen world around me, so that environmental happenings and losses are more directly felt. Things like roadkill and obviously-poisoned bees walking in a daze are no longer as easy to shake off; and the veil that others use to mask their personal pains is more transparent so that talking of the weather feels insane at moments. It's also increasingly difficult to eat "dead" processed foods and work in institutional/industrial settings where the daily harms are so apparent. In your other blogs, directly discussing collapse, there is an obvious element of grief that people discuss, but with the occult practices, it seems less obvious that (at least in our time) becoming more balanced, clear-headed, and connected to the forces around can also lead to grief and overwhelm.

I've found that just continuing the practices that brought about the awareness also helps with maintaining my balance and functionality amidst the grief. I also enjoyed a book by Martín Prechtel, The Smell of Rain on Dust, which discusses grief and praise and their relation and interconnection. I do find that experiencing grief is also a form of praise - I mourn because I care and bear witness and that care and witnessing are a form of praise. I also find that this is where religion can offer balance to occult practice; it can be a comfort to trust that there are flows of intent and power beyond me and to have humility about my place within it all. Finally, the MOE practices give something to do to help even when I cannot or will not physically intervene, which is a blessing.

JMG, I'm curious about your thoughts on increased focus, awareness, and connection leading to grief and any practices or meditation themes you'd recommend to make my way through it, or, more likely, to achieve a more functional relationship with it.

Commentariat, I'm interested in your experiences along these lines and the practices you've found that help you with grief brought on by greater awareness and sensitivity.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content: I’ve been finding, after a few months of regular practice in the GD tradition along with some baby steps into spiritual alchemy, that I’m getting less reactive. I’m much less easily lured into pointless squabbles over differing opinions on subjects from politics to metaphysics; getting into debates over such things used to be a constant bad habit of mine, and now I’m much more secure in what I believe and can just leave it be.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content: Similar to you, I actually find that this work has led me to be less reactive to others. Also, daily prioritizing and interactions seem to be easier. The grief I'm describing is less mental and more, I guess, spiritual.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content: I'm going to leave this one for the commentariat. I've experienced my share of grief in this life, but my ways of dealing with it are idiosyncratic, and I've found from experience that most other people don't find them suitable.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content:
Thank you and thanks for this space as always.
A follow up question...
I have no small amount of emotional turmoil surrounding Western European culture in general, and the effects of it's imperialism on the rest of the world, particularly the assault on indigenous peoples and imposition of monoculture, and the obsession with domination, control, and extraction of our mineral, plant, and animal relations.
I'm also, perhaps contrarily, drawn to the pantheons of my ancestors in Western Europe. When looking at the norse/anglo-saxon and greek pantheons and their stories, it is not hard to extract ideals of "might makes right" and "use wit and subterfuge to come out of agreements with more than you put in;" and even some support for viewing other beings as lesser playthings (i.e. the norse Gods hating the jotuns/giants, or the greek God's controlling and playing with human lives the way we do other beings, though that seems a categorical mistake on our part.) I'd imagine any being on the divine level viewing the last few centuries of violence enacted by Western European culture to be problematic, though my meditations on the fact just present more and more layers of complexity.
Anyway, I'm curious how do you view this - do you believe that just as there are Gods that seem impartial to human war and death, are others fine with the desolation of nature? I wonder if perhaps divinity and morality being tightly linked is a monotheistic feature best left behind in polytheism, and that worship of divine powers is more about acknowledgement of what IS rather than what Should Be. It seems the SoP maybe supports this view in that it can invoke at various points deities whose actions seem at odds with one another, and I suppose it does this in seeking balance rather than any moral alignment.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content:
First of all, most human cultures around the world and throughout time have practiced warfare, slavery, and the conquest of their neighbors, and a great many of them have wrecked their environments at least once in recorded history or recent prehistory. European cultures happen to have had the technology to do it on the grand scale in recent centuries, but that's the only thing that sets them apart. It fascinates me to watch people of European descent clinging to the conviction that there must be something unique and special about European cultures, even to the extent that they think those cultures must have been uniquely evil!

Second, as I see it, morality consists of the rules that human beings come up with in order to manage relations with other human beings. Morals don't apply to the natural world -- a tree isn't being wicked when it falls on your house and damages the roof, for example -- and they also don't apply to gods. Gods are eternal beings; they are what they are; some of them express themselves in human society by way of a moral virtue, or a set of closely related moral virtues -- think of courage and the martial virtues as expressions of Mars in human life, for example -- while others express themselves by way of things you can't call moral in any sense -- Pan and Dionysos are great examples here.

Third, the gods and goddesses of old Europe are no worse, in terms of human morality, than their equivalents in other societies. This is one reason I very strongly encourage anyone interested in mythology to read myths from at least two other cultures utterly unrelated to the one that's of personal interest. You might find it illuminating, for example, to take in some Hawai'ian mythology, say, and see what you think of the activities of Maui the trickster and Akua the war god.

replies.title: Re: Awareness and Grief
replies.content:
I appreciate your candor and these thoughts. I suppose I was making my way there slowly in separating divinity from morality, but this also clearly reveals that I've yet to shake off elements of idealism, exceptionalism, and whatever this cultural guilt is - likely a holdover from too much environmental and collapsitarian reading that bemoans humans' (particularly westerners') destructiveness.

As you recommend, I do believe it's time for me to include more history and other mythologies in my reading, and to directly explore this cultural exceptionalism and cultural guilt through journaling and more meditation.

replies.content:

comments.title: Doing daily practices in nature

Hello Sir,

Second part of the question I went from working day shift into a night shift. I used to wake up in the morning and do my daily practices. Is that still applicable i.e. wake up at 4 in the afternoon and continue on like it morning or do my daily practices before bed at say 8 a.m.

Thank you Sir

replies.title: Re: Doing daily practices in nature
replies.content:
When we sleep, our conscious mind is the part that goes dormant. That allows the various parts of the soul to recover and coordinate without having to keep continuous watch over all the conscious mind's antics. Hence the importance of getting enough sleep.

Whenever the conscious goes to sleep for a long enough time (for most of us that's about 15 minutes, though it can be trained longer) any intentions it was holding consciously, including conscious directives to the far more powerful soul, simply fade away. Fortunately, our habits, dispositions, moods, curses, and everything else lodged in the subconscious persist with a vengeance (imagine the disorientation of waking up with no sense of self or character remaining — yikes!)

Our daily practices are willful conscious intentions, which get reset every time we go to sleep. Ideally, we manage to make those requests of our soul's united power with sufficient will and discipline for them to turn into habits and dispositions that then don't get overwritten so easily. Nonetheless, renewing our commitments each time we wake up lays down those conscious intentions anew for our next set of waking hours.

By far, the most important thing is to do your practices every day, whenever you can manage to fit them in, so that they will become part of your outlook and character. If you can do them upon awakening, you will then have that many more hours with their conscious intention activated within you. It only provides a minor advantage, but, let’s face it, most of us can use all the advantage we can get!

— Christophe

Then go ahead and perform the full sequence of practices, including the banishing ritual. I've found that this works quite well.

2) I'm rarely up much before noon, and do my practices after I get up, whenever that happens to be. You can certainly do the same, and practice your magic bright and early at 5 pm.

comments.title: Migraine
comments.content:
Do you know of any outside-the-physical-plane aspects of migraine?

Obviously, neurology can do a big whammo on your thinking and abilities all by itself, but there are some common aspects of the disorder that point to the possibility that it's not a strictly physical phenomenon. Before, during, or after an attack, it is not uncommon for migraineurs to experience some fairly weird altered states of consciousness, and entopic hallucinations also go with the territory (not universal, but fairly common). I can't help but think a more complete picture of what's going on might include the astral and etheric, and that this would have implications for ways to respond to it.

Lately, I have been trying to change my approach to the whole problem... where if, say, a headache is keeping me awake at night, I will stop trying to sleep and try to re-frame the situation as an opportunity to explore... the weird landscape of consciousness that a migraine attack opens up. I go to my prayer corner and meditate with my beads. I can't claim any revelations, but the results are suggestive: about 200 repetitions in, I can move my locus of... uh... "perceived self"(??) down out of my skull into my ribcage, with the happy result that I'm aware of the pain, but I don't have to be in the middle of it, enveloped by it. Much more manageable that way.

These meditations make it seem like the pain is... more of a distraction than anything else. Not the main event. Maybe even a gate-guard. Meditating during an attack, it feels like there's an open doorway there, being obscured by pain, and that by figuring out... something (the proper way to integrate the pain? How to embrace and move through it? My vocabulary fails me here), I'd be able to go through. No idea what might be on the other side, but it doesn't feel threatening.

So I'm curious if anybody else has explored the thing-- it's so clearly not "just a headache" after all. Understandable if it's simply absent from the literature, though I'm aware that in the historical/medical writing side of things, it's not unheard-of for the (rare) migraineur to experience religious euphoria and visions as part of a migraine. Hildegard von Bingen comes to mind. Any inkling what's going on there?

replies.title: Re: Migraine
replies.content: That's fascinating. No, I haven't read anything about this before -- anybody else?

replies.title: Re: Migraine
replies.content:
I can share my personal experience with this. Migraines run in my family, I'm at least a 4th gen migraineur - my mother is actually passed out from an attack now due to the weather. However my own migraines rarely manifest with pain any longer. I still get all the ancillary effects, especially things like weird tastes and altered moods. They used to be painful and occasionally still are but they're nothing compared to how they were when I was younger or how all the other women in my family have.

One thing I started doing when they were still painful, especially when I couldn't sleep, was to get up and write out whatever would come out. I used a legal pad and wrote with a fountain pen - it sort of put me in a trance-like state. I found it didn't really matter what I wrote as long as I spent time focusing on the action of writing by hand instead of the pain and after a while I could get to where I could sleep the pain off. After some years of this I switched to doing tarot spreads instead of writing; I created a little ritual of making my bed, using a special fabric to lay the cards out on, came up with a spread that I only used when I was in that state. This seemed to be snap me into the state where I could sleep it off faster. I thought about combining the writing and the tarot while in that state to see if I could prove I was more accurate but I usually wasn't capable of that much!

Also, not medical advice, increasing my daily magnesium intake seemed to help me with the pain intensity aspects (it did nothing for the other obviously neurological effects). My mother has a tragus piercing that took away some, but not all, of her auras - she actually regrets it because they could be reliably used as indicators before the pain started!

comments.title: Short circuit - or shortcut?
comments.content:
About "short circuits" as discussed in the CosDoc: I had some difficulties to make sense of this part of the text but your essay helped a lot...

I've noticed that while the book displays short circuits as an entirely negative thing, the world seems to be full of them. Reflexes, the patellar reflex for example, are a short circuit on the material plane, maybe involving the etheric. What we call natural laws are short circuits and they seem to function without the involvement of higher planes which may be the reason why materialistic science does a not so bad job describing purely (or mostly) material things. On higher planes there are short circuits, too, but the fraction of harmful ones seems to be larger than the ones working on the lower planes only which veers more in the direction described by the CosDoc and in your essay.

One could possibly link this with Myers concept of the sub- and supra-liminal, I guess. So there are short circuits, or better circuits, which are omnipresent, rigid and robust and they are largely beyond our conscious control, although it seems to be possible to bring them into your consciousness and do "your own stuff" with them, going as far as levitation, if the accounts are at least partly correct. Then there are circuits which can be subject to our consciousness but tend to make things go haywire. And there are things largely beyond our perception of which we don't have any clue yet.

Are those rigid, robust and omnipresent circuits the work of the life swarms who came before us? Does that mean that evolutions coming after us will find conditions on higher planes, for example the astral, that are more rigid than what we experience today? Or is everything "in place" there already and the laws are just made in a way that allow for more freedom?

Thank you!

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Short circuit - or shortcut?
replies.content: It seems to me that you've just set out a fine set of meditations for yourself! That's about a month of solid work.

comments.title: The Mysterious Dodecahedrons of the Roman Empire
comments.content:
Not a question but something interesting I found today concerning Dodecahedrons on Atlas Obscura (by the way, before you travel to a new place, highly recommend you see what Atlas Obscura lists for the city or location you are visiting).

The Atlas Obscura article goes out of its way to say that no one really knows what the Romans used and valued these items for (true enough) but neither article mentions Plato's Theory of Forms which is my guess influenced whatever magical / ceremonial uses of the shapes.

Also interesting that the dodecahedrons are mostly found in the area of old Gaul.


https://www.atlasobscura.com/articles/dodecahedrons-roman-empire

https://www.livescience.com/roman-dodecahedron-discovered-belgium

replies.title: Re: The Mysterious Dodecahedrons of the Roman Empire
replies.content: Yes, I read about these! Fascinating stuff.

replies.title: Re: The Mysterious Dodecahedrons of the Roman Empire
replies.content:
Oh, those are a fun case - I remember either them or a similar Greek object type coming up in another context a couple of years back (sadly I don't have the link) precisely because some archaeologist showed one to his grandmother and she went "oh, that's a knitting(/older equivalent) tool!".

(On a related note, there was apparently a kind of round very short ground enclosure in Egyptian archaeological sites that confused researchers until one of them happened to ask one of the site workers, who promptly led them to a residence where exactly such circles were still being used millennia later to enclose chicks in such a way that their parents could step out and get food.)

- pretentious_username

comments.title: pervasive magic?
comments.content:
Hi, I'm the same person who slipped in before midnight EST a couple weeks ago (I decided to be a bit more timely this time around). You had very good advice there on cultivating skills in order to project your consciousness through the ritual. Anyway, a couple things:

First, I went ahead and did my ritual at midnight on the full moon, as I planned. It was my first attempt, and I don't know how much of a difference it made, but nothing went wrong at least. However, I experienced definite nervousness right at the beginning, so that I fumbled with my lighter (although it went fine after that), and something about it was satisfying and invigorated my mental state. I wonder if this is normal?

From this, and further reading, I'm starting to think that magic is not some cloistered, segregated arcane force (as I used to think of it), but rather something that overlaps with our reality in general. Perhaps all sorts of things we do, such as music, sex, intoxicating substances, gaming, exercise, reading, contemplation, travel, etc. all have magical qualities which are often misunderstood. Am I on the right track here?

Regards, djs.

replies.title: Re: pervasive magic?
replies.content:
1) Maybe someday I'll meet someone who doesn't get nervous in such settings, but it hasn't happened yet. If you got a sense of satisfaction and energy, that's a good start.

2) Yes, exactly! Magic is present everywhere and in all things.

comments.title: On changing thought patterns and behaviors
comments.content:
Hey there JMG, good evening,

I particularly liked how you mentioned that during a Saturn Return a lot of people are pushed to get moving and I liked that because it seems that during my first one that is exactly what is happening to me, I can no longer ignore whatever things in my life need attention, and with that in mind, instead of having to suffer my Saturn Return I think then that it is possible to make good use of it instead of suffering it by doing those things instead of delaying them.

It is not like I am old, I am a few months short of thirty, but I am not super young either and I feel that the clock keeps ticking for me to start creating the life that I want for myself if I want to be good and successful in whatever form my future career takes, specially if it needs to be a 180 degrees from where I am now which is no easy thing because that involved the changing of oneself.

1) I assume taking decisions during the couple of years of a Saturn Return isn't the best of ideas correct? How is this time best made use of instead?

2) Is doing a candle working to Saturn a good idea or does that make things worse?

replies.title: Re: On changing thought patterns and behaviors
replies.content:
1) Spend the time cleaning up messes and fixing things about yourself that you know you need to work on. Oh, and dealing with what Saturn throws at you!

2) It depends very much on what you want to accomplish by that. If you just want to burn a candle, recite the Orphic hymn, and let Saturn know that you're doing your best to pay attention and to get your act together, that's probably beneficial. Don't ask for favors, though.
